text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC — FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled surveillance / maintenance therapy completion

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Margaret Osei is a 55-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), bilateral, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier, presenting today for her 36-month post-chemotherapy surveillance visit. This visit also marks the planned completion of niraparib maintenance therapy, which she has been receiving at 100 mg daily for approximately 34 months (with an earlier 28-day interruption for Grade 3 thrombocytopenia prior to dose reduction).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with FIGO IIIC (pT3c pN0 pM0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovaries, bilateral, with peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, and ascites (~3.5 L). Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74) pathogenic variant was identified on germline testing at the time of diagnosis. Her tumor was also HRD-positive (HRD score 54) with somatic BRCA1 allelic loss (two-hit BRCA1 inactivation). TP53 p.R248W somatic mutation was identified; TMB was low (5 mut/Mb), MSS.

She underwent primary debulking surgery (TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy) with R0 resection — complete gross cytoreduction with no visible residual disease. Surgical pathology confirmed STIC lesion in the left fimbriated end as the likely site of origin. All 27 lymph nodes sampled were negative.

She received 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) / paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, later dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 from Cycle 4 onward due to Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy). She achieved a complete response: CA-125 normalized to 11 U/mL and post-treatment CT showed no evidence of disease.

Niraparib maintenance was initiated at an individualized starting dose of 200 mg daily. She developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelet count 41 x10^9/L) at week 6, necessitating a 28-day hold followed by resumption at 100 mg daily. On the reduced dose, she has had stable low-grade thrombocytopenia (Grade 1, platelets generally 128–143 x10^9/L), manageable Grade 0-1 nausea and fatigue, and no further high-grade hematologic events. She has remained in complete remission throughout the maintenance period; the most recent surveillance CT (at ~24 months post-chemotherapy) showed no evidence of recurrence and CA-125 has been consistently normal (range 8–11 U/mL).

Today she presents to review labs, complete her final niraparib prescription, and discuss the transition plan to long-term surveillance. She took her last niraparib dose as of today.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Denies fevers, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Mild fatigue, she rates it 1/10, baseline for her at this point. No night sweats.
- GI: Mild intermittent nausea, primarily in the mornings — she attributes this to niraparib and anticipates improvement now that she is stopping. Denies vomiting, early satiety, abdominal pain, bloating, or change in bowel habits. No rectal bleeding.
- GU: Reports vaginal dryness, managed with topical vaginal estrogen gel (local application); no dyspareunia-related concerns presently. No urinary symptoms.
- Neurologic: Peripheral neuropathy from prior paclitaxel fully resolved; denies tingling, numbness, or balance difficulties. Reports mild intermittent difficulty with word retrieval and short-term memory, consistent with post-chemotherapy cognitive effects ('chemo brain') she first noted during the 12-month visit — not significantly impairing and no worse. No headache.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild arthralgias in bilateral hands and knees, not clearly chemotherapy-related; she wonders if this is menopausal arthralgia. No joint swelling or warmth.
- Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure has been well-controlled at home.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
- Psychiatric: Reports good mood overall. Some situational anxiety about completing maintenance therapy — 'what if something comes back now that I stop the pill?' — addressed in counseling during this visit.
- Heme: No unusual bruising or bleeding. No petechiae.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm (regular), Temp 36.7°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 69 kg (stable), ECOG Performance Status: 0.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversational.
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary chains.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar without hernia. No palpable organomegaly. No guarding or rigidity. No ascites on percussion. No palpable masses.
Pelvic (limited exam): Vaginal vault intact and well-healed. No palpable pelvic masses. No cuff tenderness.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses intact.
Neurologic: Grossly intact. Sensation normal in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Gait normal. No focal deficits.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
- CA-125: 11 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL) — normal, stable
- CBC:
  - WBC: 5.1 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1, stable/improving from prior 11.9)
  - Platelets: 141 x10^9/L (Grade 1, stable)
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Within normal limits; creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, eGFR >60, LFTs normal
- HE4: Not repeated at this visit

---

IMAGING:
Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 3 months prior, at the 24-month surveillance timepoint): No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, no ascites, no pelvic or abdominal masses, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions. Sustained complete remission. No new imaging obtained today; next surveillance CT planned in approximately 9–12 months per updated surveillance schedule.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, bilateral, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant — COMPLETE SUSTAINED REMISSION at 36 months post-chemotherapy.

Ms. Osei has achieved a durable, complete clinical and radiographic response following:
   - R0 primary debulking surgery
   - 6 cycles carboplatin/paclitaxel (with paclitaxel dose reduction for neuropathy)
   - ~34 months niraparib maintenance (Zejula) at individualized/reduced dose of 100 mg daily

This is a very gratifying outcome. Her germline BRCA1 status and HRD-positive tumor biology were strong predictors of platinum sensitivity and PARP inhibitor benefit, consistent with SOLO1 trial data demonstrating markedly prolonged progression-free survival in this population. We have now completed the planned ~3-year maintenance course.

Plan:
   - Final niraparib dose taken today. Prescription not renewed. Discussed that this is expected and planned — not a sign of failure.
   - Counseled patient on transition anxiety around stopping maintenance: reassured that the 3-year benchmark reflects the studied and approved duration; ongoing protective benefit beyond this point is less clear, and cumulative toxicity risk (hematologic) does not favor indefinite extension outside a clinical trial.
   - Discussed that recurrence risk, while real, is most concentrated in the first 2 years and her trajectory to date has been excellent.

2. SIDE EFFECT REVIEW — NIRAPARIB DISCONTINUATION:
   - Thrombocytopenia (Grade 1, platelets 141 x10^9/L today): Anticipate gradual recovery of platelet counts over the weeks following discontinuation. Repeat CBC in 4–6 weeks.
   - Nausea/fatigue: Expected to resolve or significantly improve after stopping.
   - Mild anemia (Hgb 12.1): Stable and slowly improving; likely to continue recovering off niraparib. Will recheck in 4–6 weeks.

3. SURVEILLANCE PLAN — TRANSITION TO LONG-TERM FOLLOW-UP:
   - CA-125 every 3 months through year 5 post-diagnosis, then every 6 months thereafter.
   - CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: Annually or symptom-directed. Next scan in approximately 9–12 months from today.
   - Clinical exam every 6 months.
   - Discussed symptoms of recurrence: new/worsening abdominal pain or bloating, early satiety, increasing abdominal girth, urinary urgency or frequency not explained by UTI, unexplained weight loss, fatigue disproportionate to activity. Patient instructed to call the clinic for any new or concerning symptoms between scheduled visits rather than waiting for the next appointment.

4. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS:
   - Topical vaginal estrogen continued (local application, minimal systemic absorption, acceptable in this setting per gynecologic oncology guidance).
   - Venlafaxine for hot flashes: Patient reports this has been helpful. Continue at current dose.

5. COGNITIVE CHANGES ('CHEMO BRAIN'):
   - Mild cognitive symptoms (word retrieval, short-term memory) noted at 12-month visit have not worsened and are not functionally impairing. She is fully active as a high school biology teacher.
   - No formal neuropsychological testing indicated at this time.
   - Encouraged aerobic exercise, adequate sleep, and cognitive engagement as supportive measures.

6. BRCA1 FAMILY IMPLICATIONS:
   - Patient's daughter (age 27) has undergone BRCA1 genetic testing and was found to carry the familial BRCA1 pathogenic variant. She has been referred to high-risk surveillance and risk-reduction counseling. No further action required on our end at this visit.
   - Patient encouraged to remind any other first-degree relatives who have not yet been tested.

7. BONE HEALTH:
   - Patient is postmenopausal and on aromatase suppression (surgical menopause); dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan overdue — order placed today. Calcium 1,200 mg daily and vitamin D 1,000 IU daily recommended if not already taking.

8. RETURN TO CARE:
   - Follow-up in 3 months for CA-125 check and clinical assessment. Patient has clinic contact information and knows to call with any concerns.
   - Patient verbalized understanding of and agreement with the plan. Questions addressed. She expressed gratitude and is in good spirits.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 40 minutes.

---

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gynecologic Oncology
